abstract:
Sn-based materials are recognized as highly promising catalysts for the selective electrochemical reduction of CO$_2$ (CO$_2$RR). However, identifying the precise structures of catalytic intermediates and the specific surface species that drive this process remains a significant challenge. In this study, we developed a series of single-Sn-atom catalysts with well-defined coordination environments to serve as model systems for investigating electrochemical CO$_2$RR reactivity. Our findings demonstrate that the activity and selectivity for formic acid (HCOOH) production are directly correlated with Sn(IV)-N$_4$ moieties that are axially coordinated with oxygen (O-Sn-N$_4$). The optimized O-Sn-N$_4$ catalyst achieved a maximum HCOOH Faradaic efficiency of 89.4% and a partial current density ($j_{HCOOH}$) of 74.8 mA cm$^{-2}$ at -1.0 V vs RHE.

To probe the reaction mechanism, we employed an integrated suite of *operando* techniques—including X-ray absorption spectroscopy, attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS), Raman spectroscopy, and $^{119}$Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy. This approach allowed us to capture surface-bound bidentate tin carbonate species during the reaction and accurately characterize the electronic and coordination states of the single-Sn-atom sites under operating conditions. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further reveal that the O-Sn-N$_4$ sites favor the formation of Sn-O-CO$_2$ species. This configuration modulates the adsorption of reactive intermediates and significantly lowers the energy barrier for the hydrogenation of *OCHO. In contrast, Sn-N$_4$ sites preferentially form *COOH, leading to different product pathways. These results clarify how axial oxygen coordination facilitates the efficient conversion of CO$_2$ to HCOOH at the atomic level.
